Feature Carveyance

Low and Behold

How the Steinwinter Supercargo — the lowest truck ever built — slid clean under its own trailer, dazzled the world in 1983, secretly starred in your favorite absurd 80s TV show, and then vanished off the face of the earth thanks to a single line of legislation.

Engineering  ·  1983, Stuttgart  ·  A Beautiful Failure

Here is an iron law of 1980s television: no matter how square the jaw of the leading man, the real star of the show always had wheels, rotors, or a turbocharger. The decade handed us a parade of impossibly macho heroes, and then quietly let their machines steal every single scene out from under them.

Think about it. Knight Rider sold itself on David Hasselhoff’s feathered hair, but the actual icon was KITT — a 1982 Pontiac Trans Am with a sweeping red eye and the voice of a faintly judgmental butler. The A-Team had four fugitive commandos, yet the thing every kid actually wanted was the black-and-grey GMC Vandura van, with its single red stripe and that rooftop spoiler. Airwolf gave us brooding pilots; we only ever cared about the Bell 222 helicopter cosplaying as a supersonic stealth weapon. The Wraith handed a 21-year-old Charlie Sheen the keys to the afterlife, but the eternal soul of that film was the Dodge M4S Turbo Interceptor — a Chrysler-and-PPG concept pace car so otherworldly it was cast, fittingly, as a literal ghost. And the most famous example of all nearly didn’t happen on wheels: Back to the Future originally imagined its time machine as a refrigerator, until someone realized that (a) a freshly launched, gullwinged DeLorean DMC-12 looked infinitely cooler, and (b) it was perhaps unwise to plant “climb inside a fridge” ideas in the minds of America’s children. The DeLorean became immortal. The car, frankly, got far more out of that arrangement than the movie did.

That Airwolf helicopter has earned its footnote right here, because the truth is stranger and sadder than anything in the script. After the cameras stopped rolling, the actual modified Bell 222 was stripped of its fake missile pods, shipped to Germany, and put to honest work as an air ambulance. On the 6th of June, 1992 — having just flown a little girl with terrible burns from Berlin to the specialist unit in Cologne, a genuine mercy run, a real life saved — it ran into fog and a thunderstorm on the way home and went into the side of a mountain, killing all three crew aboard. The wolf in sheep’s clothing spent its final day as an actual angel of mercy. I did not expect to get a lump in my throat researching a truck article. And yet.

Which brings me, by way of the single most unhinged entry in the entire genre, to the reason we’re all here. In 1987 the gloriously bonkers Glen A. Larson — the showrunner behind Knight Rider and Battlestar Galactica — handed NBC a series called The Highwayman. It was, and I mean this as the highest possible compliment, completely incoherent: a near-future world of federal “Highwaymen” policing the lawless outlands, summed up by roughly everyone who saw it as “Mad Max meets Knight Rider.” Flash Gordon himself, Sam J. Jones, starred. But of course he wasn’t the star. The star was the truck — a colossal black big rig named Stealth that could turn invisible, hid a Lotus Esprit in its tail for the chase scenes (you genuinely cannot go wrong dropping an Esprit into anything), and whose bullet-shaped cab detached and flew off as a helicopter. As one does.

And here is the detail that made me want to write all of this down. That preposterous television truck — the low, flat, slung-under-the-trailer one that looked exactly like the tug that hauls your jet to the gate — was not pure fantasy. It was heavily, unmistakably “inspired by” a real machine: a real, road-going, Mercedes-powered, gloriously doomed vehicle that a German engineer actually built in 1983 and showed to the entire automotive world. It was called the Steinwinter Supercargo, and it remains, to this day, the lowest truck ever built. This is its story — how it worked, why it was brilliant, and why one paragraph of paperwork erased it from existence.

• • •

Design Philosophy

The Loophole That Launched a Truck

To understand why a sane and serious engineer would build a truck barely taller than a coffee table, you first have to understand the rulebook of 1980s European haulage — because the Supercargo wasn’t born from madness. It was born from a loophole, which is a far more dangerous thing.

At the time, European law capped the total length of an articulated truck at roughly 18 metres. Full stop. Cab, engine, trailer, the whole lot. The trouble was that in a conventional rig, the towering cab and its diesel sat out front and devoured three or four of those precious metres before you’d loaded a single pallet. Hauliers were, in effect, paying to chauffeur a driver and a lump of machinery around in nearly a quarter of their legal envelope.

Manfred Steinwinter, an engineer from Stuttgart, looked at that wasted space the way a chess player eyes a hanging queen. What if, he reasoned, the cab and engine didn’t sit ahead of the trailer at all — but completely beneath it? Flatten the entire tractor down to about the height of a sports car, slide it under the cargo box, and all that dead frontal space simply ceases to exist. The fifth-wheel coupling would ride on the roof of the cab; the trailer would stretch nearly the full legal length.

The numbers were intoxicating. A standard rig might give you around 12 metres of usable load length; the Supercargo promised roughly 18 metres and a cavernous 150 cubic metres of volume — as much as a third more cargo inside the very same legal box, with Steinwinter’s own brochures happy to imply even more. And because he’d deleted the great brick wall of a traditional cab, aerodynamic drag — the silent thief that drinks a long-haul truck’s diesel — would fall off a cliff. More freight and less fuel, from the same footprint. On paper, it was the closest the haulage industry had ever come to a genuine free lunch.

Technical Anatomy

The Anatomy of the Lowest Truck on Earth

This was no fibreglass show-stand toy, either. Unveiled at the 1983 Frankfurt Motor Show — and yes, the year was 1983, please mentally correct anyone who insists otherwise — the Supercargo 20.40 was a fully engineered, running machine, and Steinwinter had talked Mercedes-Benz into donating a chassis and the good mechanical hardware to prove it.

The headline figure was the height: 1,170 millimetres. One metre and seventeen centimetres. That is lower than a great many sedans of the day, and roughly level with the roofline of a Porsche 911 — except this particular 911-height object was engineered to drag forty tonnes down the autobahn.

The real engineering challenge was a packaging puzzle from hell: how do you fit a full V8, a 16-speed gearbox, the driveline, the exhaust, and two human beings into something the height of a coffee table? The functional 1983 prototype — the one that was actually built and driven — answered with a Mercedes-Benz OM422: a 14.6-litre diesel V8, mounted centrally just behind the seats for balance and breathing through a ZF 16-speed manual. In its naturally aspirated and turbocharged (OM422 A) forms, that engine made somewhere between 276 and 330 horsepower and a thumping 753 lb-ft of torque — the kind of low, endless shove a loaded truck lives on.

There’s an asterisk here worth knowing, because the engine question is exactly where half the internet trips over itself. Later iterations and concept versions proposed the bigger-hitting Mercedes-Benz OM442 LA — a twin-turbocharged, intercooled V8 — to reliably serve up a full 400 horsepower. Steinwinter happily used both across his modular designs: the workaday OM422 in the running prototype, the muscular OM442 LA in the grander proposals — which is precisely why you’ll see this one truck quoted at anything from 276 to 400 hp depending on who’s telling the story. Both, for the record, are card-carrying members of Mercedes’ bombproof OM400 family of V8 diesels — the same lineage that powered the marque’s heavy trucks, buses, and fire engines for years, and soldiered on in marine, industrial, and military duty (sold in Detroit Diesel/MTU guise as the Series 183). In other words, whichever heart it happened to be wearing, the Supercargo’s engine was a thoroughly proven workhorse. It was only the body wrapped around it that had lost its mind.

To make a vehicle this long and this low usable at all, Steinwinter threw the entire toolbox at it: fully pneumatic air suspension on every axle, so the truck could kneel down to wriggle under a trailer and then rise to lock onto the coupling; rear-axle steering as well as front, so the enormous thing could still thread a narrow European street; ABS; and a limited-slip differential. Inside, the driver reclined almost horizontally in fighter-jet fashion, sunk into Recaro buckets behind a wraparound dash, with a compact sleeper berth and even a small wardrobe tucked behind the seats. It was, somehow, equal parts race car and studio apartment.

And oh, the interior — this is the part I cannot get over. For a freight vehicle, the cabin was borderline decadent: trimmed wall-to-wall in warm tan leather and laid out less like a lorry than like something limbering up for a timed lap of the Nürburgring. The driver sat low and reclined, nested in deeply bolstered Recaro buckets — the exact kind of seat engineered to pin you in place through fast corners, which is a marvellous thing to fit to a forty-tonne truck that should, under no circumstances, ever be taking fast corners. Around it: a wraparound bank of switchgear, proper instrument dials, a sleeper berth, that natty little wardrobe. And my single favourite detail — alongside the driver sat not one but two passenger seats, side by side, like a plush little sidecar. I have decided, with absolutely no supporting evidence, that this was for family road trips: three across in the lowest truck on Earth, watching the world slide by at axle height. Frankly? Sounds like a blast.

The Road Test

When Physics Sent Its Regards

And then they actually drove it, and the universe began filing objections.

The first and most damning was visibility — or the catastrophic lack of it. A conventional trucker sits two or three metres up, surveying the road like a captain on a bridge, reading the traffic far ahead. The Supercargo’s driver sat at the eye level of a Lotus, except with a wall of cargo looming behind and above him. He could barely see over the boot of the hatchback directly ahead, let alone anticipate the brake lights five cars up the motorway. On a road full of forty-tonne consequences, that is not a quirk. That is a coffin specification.

The second problem was physics quietly doing its homework. In a normal artic, the trailer’s mass trails obediently behind the cab and the handling is broadly predictable. In the Supercargo, thirty-odd tonnes of cargo perched directly on top of a tractor that was short, light, and low. The instant the road turned slick, that top-heavy, badly distributed mass wanted to swap ends — a jackknife waiting for an excuse — and for good measure, testers reported stubborn understeer. Worse still, through tighter corners the long container up top would swing wide, drifting out into the oncoming lane like a tail that hadn’t been told it was attached to a dog.

And then there was the cabin, which committed the cardinal sin of being genuinely miserable to occupy. With a 14.6-litre V8 hammering away centimetres behind your skull, and none of the cooling airflow that a tall conventional cab quietly provides, the cockpit became a hot, deafening little oven capable of wringing out a driver within minutes. The low windscreen, meanwhile, caught the full barrage of spray, grit, and slush flung up by every other vehicle on the road. And you couldn’t even stand up to stretch your back. Aerospace cockpit on the way out; sweatbox on the way home.

A 911-height truck dragging forty tonnes — brilliant on paper, terrifying in the rain.

The End

Death by a Single Paragraph

By rights, the Supercargo should have either been fixed or quietly forgotten. Instead it got something far more poignant: it was killed by the very rulebook that had created it.

Manfred Steinwinter, to his enormous credit, did not go gently. He reimagined the platform as a modular kit — swap the trailer for a container module, or a luxury coach, or an RV, or even a double-decker bus with the driver tucked below and the passengers riding above. He sketched it as a mobile infirmary. He courted fresh backers; DAF and Isuzu each had a curious look and then drifted away, in part because the thing simply wasn’t that much thriftier than an ordinary truck once you hung a real trailer off it. Mercedes never took the plunge. And Germany’s road-safety authorities, eyeing a cockpit with essentially no crumple zone — and harboring a very reasonable fear that, in a frontal impact, the trailer could guillotine forward and flatten the cab — declined to wave it through to production. And look, some small, reckless corner of me wants to file an objection of its own here, because when a machine looks this cool, surely safety can occasionally be asked to ride in the (admittedly nonexistent) back seat — a crumple zone feels like such a small price to pay for this much awesomeness. The German authorities, being sober professionals rather than excitable car writers, disagreed.

But the true executioner wore a suit and carried a clipboard. Around 1990, European legislators rewrote the length rules: the new ceiling became 18.75 metres of total length, with a generous, guaranteed loading length baked right in. In a single stroke, an ordinary, sensible, sit-up-high truck could now swallow nearly as much cargo as the Supercargo — minus the sauna, the blindness, and the jackknife roulette. The loophole that Steinwinter had built an entire vehicle to exploit evaporated overnight, and with it went the only real reason the Supercargo had to exist.

To the best of anyone’s knowledge, exactly one was ever built. It was modified, re-tested, and then set aside, and over the following decades it slipped quietly into obscurity — no museum hall, no auction-block headline, just a scattering of archival photographs and grainy footage of the lowest truck the world has ever seen.

The Verdict

The Real Star, After All

Here’s the thing, though, and it’s exactly why I opened with a parade of television vehicles. The Steinwinter Supercargo failed at the single job it was built to do. It never hauled freight for a living, never filled a motorway, never earned anyone a deutschmark. By every cold metric of the trucking business, it was a flop.

And yet I can sit here and tell you, in loving detail, what it was and how it worked some four decades later — while the perfectly sensible, perfectly profitable trucks that shared the 1983 Frankfurt floor with it have vanished from memory entirely. Nobody writes love letters to a well-optimized cabover. We remember the swing-for-the-fences lunatics, every time.

And the Supercargo’s real second act, fittingly, never happened on the highway at all. It happened on television, as the muse for The Highwayman’s impossible truck — the low, flat, slung-under machine that an entire generation of kids pointed at and said, I want that one. Which is the most gloriously 1980s ending imaginable. The macho hero gets top billing; the vehicle gets the immortality. Manfred Steinwinter built a truck that was too clever, too low, and too early for the real world — so the real world said no, and pop culture said absolutely, put it on at eight o’clock. Lo, and behold.

One Last Theory

The Airport-Bar Hypothesis

Now, I want to be crystal clear about this next bit: I have found absolutely no evidence for what I am about to suggest. None whatsoever. But it does not take much of an imaginative leap, and I’d like to walk you through it anyway, because it’s too good not to. Picture Manfred Steinwinter at an airport — possibly after a drink, possibly after several — watching a Hunslet ATT77 air towing tractor go about its quiet, unglamorous business. The ATT77 is a low, flat, impossibly squat machine built for exactly one job: calmly shoving the better part of a million pounds of fully fuelled 747 around the tarmac like it’s nudging a shopping trolley. And I can absolutely imagine a tired, faintly tipsy engineer staring at that thing and thinking: hang on — why is the most efficient heavy-hauling shape on this entire airfield not allowed out on the motorway?

Look at the two of them side by side and tell me I’m crazy. The same cab-forward, ground-hugging, flat-as-a-coin profile; the same trick of tucking a small, purposeful tractor beneath an enormous load. Take that airport-tug genius, point it at the freight industry, wrap it in tan leather and a Mercedes V8, and you have more or less talked yourself straight into the Steinwinter Supercargo.

And here is the part that makes the whole half-baked theory worth entertaining. That very same Hunslet ATT77 went on to a spectacular second life of its own — not in trucking, but on film. Strip it down, armour it up, bolt on some menace, and the humble airport tug becomes the M577 armoured personnel carrier that Ripley and the Colonial Marines come tearing out of in James Cameron’s 1986 masterpiece, Aliens — one of the most outrageously badass vehicles ever committed to the silver screen.

So follow the thread with me, evidence be damned: a low-slung airport tractor may have quietly inspired the lowest truck the world has ever built, and then turned right around and became the baddest ride in a science-fiction classic. Two of the coolest machines of the entire decade, possibly descended from the very same unglamorous contraption bolted together to shove jumbo jets around a runway. I cannot prove a single word of it. I absolutely refuse to let that stop me.
